coredata.dc:title: Antibacterial potentials of carbon dots immobilized on chitosan and glass surfaces
coredata.dc:creator: Najmalden Ghaibullah Ghaibullah, Yanar
Foto, Egemen
Ozdemir, Naciye
Zilifdar Foto, Fatma
Arslan, Gulsin
Sargin, Idris
coredata.dc:description: Due to their antibacterial activity, chitosan‑carbon dot composites possess great potential for pharmaceuticals, medicine, and food preservation. Conducting a comprehensive study of the interactions between chitosan, carbon dots, and bacteria is crucial to understanding the processes behind applying these composites. This study aimed to immobilize carbon dots (C-dots) synthesized from Elaeagnus angustifolia fruits on chitosan and glass microbeads' surfaces, to characterize the test materials obtained after synthesis and immobilization, and to investigate their antibacterial potentials. C-dot synthesis was carried out from water extract in an acidic medium with the help of microwave irradiation, and their structural and optical properties were characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR, UV–vis, Zeta potential, and fluorescence methods. The surface of the glass microbeads was first activated and functionalized with surface amine groups with a silaning agent. C-dots were immobilized on both glass and chitosan microbeads using a crosslinking agent. Antibacterial potentials of nine different test materials, obtained before or after immobilization, were evaluated both qualitatively (MIC and MBC) and quantitatively (GI50) on E. coli, S. typhimurium, B. subtilis, and S. aureus, with the standard broth microdilution method. FT-IR and SEM-EDX analyses showed that C-dots were immobilized on chitosan (˂1 mm) and glass (˂100 μm) microbead surfaces. C-dots reduced the cell viability by ~25 % on S. typhimurium and B. subtilis (MIC = 25 mg/mL). It was also found that the highest antibacterial effect was recorded for C-dots-glass microbeads, which had a toxic effect of 43 % on S. aureus. In addition, binding C-dots to glass microbeads increased the antibacterial effect selectively in Gram-positive bacteria, while binding to chitosan microbeads was effective in all bacteria. The study showed that the antibacterial potential of C-dots-chitosan microbeads is more effective than C-dots-glass microbeads. C-dots could be used as carbon-based nanomaterials in antibacterial surface preparation once immobilized.

FT-IR spectrum of the carbon dots.

The carbon dots had a broad UV–vis absorbance range, with small peaks at 273 and 351 nm (a). They exhibited an excitation-dependent fluorescence emission (b), and their photoluminescence was pH- and solvent-sensitive (c, d). The fluorescence intensity of the carbon dots was not significantly affected in the presence of NaCl (e). XRD patterns of the carbon dots (f).

head.ce:title: Antibacterial potentials of carbon dots immobilized on chitosan and glass surfaces

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Due to their antibacterial activity, chitosan‑carbon dot composites possess great potential for pharmaceuticals, medicine, and food preservation. Conducting a comprehensive study of the interactions between chitosan, carbon dots, and bacteria is crucial to understanding the processes behind applying these composites. This study aimed to immobilize carbon dots (C-dots) synthesized from Elaeagnus angustifolia fruits on chitosan and glass microbeads' surfaces, to characterize the test materials obtained after synthesis and immobilization, and to investigate their antibacterial potentials.

C-dot synthesis was carried out from water extract in an acidic medium with the help of microwave irradiation, and their structural and optical properties were characterized by TEM, XRD, FT-IR, UV–vis, Zeta potential, and fluorescence methods. The surface of the glass microbeads was first activated and functionalized with surface amine groups with a silaning agent. C-dots were immobilized on both glass and chitosan microbeads using a crosslinking agent. Antibacterial potentials of nine different test materials, obtained before or after immobilization, were evaluated both qualitatively (MIC and MBC) and quantitatively (GI50) on E. coli, S. typhimurium, B. subtilis, and S. aureus, with the standard broth microdilution method.

FT-IR and SEM-EDX analyses showed that C-dots were immobilized on chitosan (˂1 mm) and glass (˂100 μm) microbead surfaces. C-dots reduced the cell viability by ~25 % on S. typhimurium and B. subtilis (MIC = 25 mg/mL). It was also found that the highest antibacterial effect was recorded for C-dots-glass microbeads, which had a toxic effect of 43 % on S. aureus. In addition, binding C-dots to glass microbeads increased the antibacterial effect selectively in Gram-positive bacteria, while binding to chitosan microbeads was effective in all bacteria. The study showed that the antibacterial potential of C-dots-chitosan microbeads is more effective than C-dots-glass microbeads. C-dots could be used as carbon-based nanomaterials in antibacterial surface preparation once immobilized.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan‑carbon dot composites have gained significant attention due to their wide-spectrum antibacterial activity, offering a variety of applications in pharmaceuticals, medicine, and food preservation [1,2]. Chitosan is a copolymer of glucosamine and N-acetylglucosamine monomers linked by glycosidic β(1 → 4) bonds. Chitosan is a deacetylated form of chitin, altered by enzymes or a hot alkaline medium. Extraction conditions, chitin source, and deacetylation method determine its properties [3]. With appropriate technological methods, Chitosan can be transformed into microspheres, microparticles, and nanoparticle forms. The choice of physical form is determined by the needs of the system required for specific applications [4]. As a cationic carbohydrate polymer, chitosan exhibits potent antibacterial activity against Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria [5]. The bactericidal and bacteriostatic properties of chitosan are primarily due to interactions between its –NH2 groups and bacterial cell wall components, such as negatively charged groups, metal ions, or genetic material within the cell [6]. However, interactions between bacterial cells and chitosan are complex and depend on source, synthesis conditions, pH, temperature, and concentration [7].

Carbon dots are bio-compatible and fluorescent nanomaterials. They are easily synthesized from synthetic or biological sources and are actively researched due to their strong fluorescence when exposed to UV radiation [8]. Unique properties of carbon dots, such as water solubility, large surface area, surface functionalities, and π-domains from their graphitic core structure, enable effective interaction with organic molecules, metal ions, and living cells [9,10]. Carbon dots have been found to exhibit antibacterial properties due to their inherent characteristics [11]. They have been studied for bioimaging, biosensors, chemical sensing, photocatalytic hydrogen generation, enzyme immobilization, catalysis, and active food packaging [12,13]. The latest research focuses on exploring the antibacterial potential of carbon dots [14,15].

Carbon dots have antibacterial properties due to physical damage, oxidative stress, and metabolic inhibition. Positively charged carbon dots interact with bacterial membranes and cause their disruption [2,16,17]. Nitrogen-doped carbon dots have higher positive charge density, improving antimicrobial activity [18]. Limited research has been done on the antibacterial effect of carbon dots with negative or no charge [19]. When excited by UV lights, carbon dots produce reactive oxygen species and free radicals that kill bacteria by oxidizing intracellular substances and disrupting intracellular structures. They differ from traditional antibacterial agents [20]. Carbon dots of appropriate size could disrupt bacterial mechanisms by penetrating cells [21]. Carbon dots' properties, antibacterial activity, and biocompatibility depend on the carbon source and synthesis techniques. Thus, researchers actively explore biological substances for synthesis [22,23].

Chitosan and carbon dots have a collaborative antibacterial effect [24–26]. A study assessed their effectiveness against E. coli using carbon dots-chitosan and lanthanum oxide systems. Doping chitosan and carbon dots enhanced the antibacterial properties of lanthanum oxide nanorods [27]. Another study used a nanocomposite made of carbon dots, hematite, and hydroxypropyl cellulose cross-linked chitosan for drug delivery, sunlight-assisted catalysis, and antimicrobial tests [2]. That nanocomposite exhibited antimicrobial activity against bacteria (S. aureus, E. coli) and fungi (Aspergillus niger, Candida albicans). Wen et al. (2023) developed food packaging films with carbon dots and chitosan to kill bacteria. These films produced reactive oxygen species that stopped the growth of E. coli and S. aureus under 405 nm light [1]. Chen et al. (2023) developed a chitosan/carbon dots composite film to preserve pork and blueberries. The film showed enhanced antibacterial, mechanical, and UV barrier properties compared to plain chitosan films [28].

Studying the interactions between chitosan‑carbon dots composites and bacteria is crucial to optimize their production and advance our knowledge of their potential applications in biomedical or active food packaging. This paper reports on the antibacterial tests of chitosan microbeads with/without carbon dots. We also evaluated the antibacterial potential of carbon dots-coated glass microbeads and free carbon dots. The carbon dots were synthesized from oleaster fruit extract using microwave irradiation. The antibacterial tests were conducted on E. coli, S. typhimurium, B. subtilis, and S. aureus. Qualitative and quantitative methods, including Minimum Inhibition Concentration (MIC), Minimum Bactericidal Concentration (MBC), and 50 % growth inhibition concentration (GI50) values, were used to assess the efficacy of the test materials.

Oleaster fruit (Elaeagnus angustifolia L.) powder (their skin and seeds were removed) was used to synthesize carbon dots via microwave-assisted synthesis. During the microwave treatment, sulfuric acid (Merck) was used to carbonize the water extract of oleaster fruits. NaOH (Merck) was to neutralize the excess sulfuric acid in the medium after the microwave treatment. Quinine hemisulphate monohydrate salt (98 %, Bioreagent, suitable for fluorescence, Sigma-Aldrich) was used as a reference in quantum yield calculations of the carbon dots.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots-coated glass microbeads
ce:section.ce:para: Glass microbeads (Sigma-Aldrich, glass beads, acid washed, G4649-100G, ≤106 μm) were washed with ethanol (absolute, Supelco, Merck) and used in the experiments. A mixture of sulfuric acid (Merck, 95–98 %, concentrated) and hydrogen peroxide (30 %, for analysis Emsure, ISO, Merck) solution was used for the surface activation of the glass microbeads. (3-aminopropyl)triethoxysilane (APTES) (99 %, Sigma-Aldrich) in toluene (Emsure, ACS, ISO, Reag., Merck) was used for the amino-functionalization of the surface-activated glass microbeads. A fluorescent xanthene dye (rhodamine B isothiocyanate, Merck) in dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO, Fisher Chemical) labeled amino-functionalized glass microbeads before the fluorescence microscopy analysis. Glutaraldehyde (Merck, aqueous solution, 25 %) immobilized carbon dots on the glass microbeads.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots-coated chitosan microbeads
ce:section.ce:para: Low molecular weight chitosan (448869-250G, Sigma-Aldrich) was used to prepare chitosan microbeads. Acetic acid (Merck) solution was used to dissolve chitosan. Chitosan gel beads were formed in a medium containing methanol (Merck), water, and NaOH (Sigma-Aldrich). Glutaraldehyde was used to cross-link chitosan microbeads and to immobilize carbon dots on the chitosan microbeads.

Müller Hinton Broth (MHB, Condalab, for microbiology) was used to prepare liquid bacteria cultures, serial dilutions in the MIC determination stages, and as a negative control. Müller Hinton Agar II (MHA, Condalab) was used in solid bacteria cultures and the cultivation stages of MBK studies. Ampicillin and tetracycline were used as positive controls in antibacterial studies. Ethanol (70 %, Merck), sterile syringe filter (Sigma-Aldrich) with a 0.22 μm pore size, and the autoclave (Tomy SX 500-E) were used to ensure aseptic conditions.

Carbon dots were synthesized from the water extract of oleaster fruits (E. angustifolia) (the skin and seeds were removed, not used) in an acidic environment with the help of microwave irradiation. In the extraction procedure, 10.0 g of oleaster fruit in 600.0 mL of distilled water was agitated at 60 °C for 3 h in an incubator and then filtrated (Whatman, 42). The filtrate was collected and used as a carbon precursor in the synthesis process.

Preliminary experiments were conducted to optimize the carbon dots synthesis conditions in a microwave oven (CEM Mars 5). The microwave irradiation procedure was repeated at varying conditions: concentrated sulfuric acid solution/distilled water ratios (1.0/9.0, 1.5/8.5, 2.0/8.0, 2.5/7.5, mL/mL), microwave energy levels (400, 800, 1600 W), microwave irradiation time (1, 3, 5, 7, 10 min.). In a typical synthesis procedure, the following conditions were followed: concentrated sulfuric acid solution/distilled water ratio 1.0/9.0 (mL/mL), microwave energy levels (800 W), and microwave irradiation time (5 min.).

NaOH was added to the reaction vessel after the microwave treatment to neutralize the acid. Once the neutralization was achieved, the solution phase was separated by centrifugation (12,000 rpm, 10 min.). The supernatant was collected and dialyzed against distilled water in a dialysis tubing (Thermo Scientific SnakeSkin, 3500 MWCO). After the dialysis, the carbon dot solutions were heated at 50 °C to evaporate the water (not till to the dryness). Finally, the wet carbon dots were dried by lyophilization.

The morphological, structural, and optical properties of carbon dots were studied by fluorescence (Perkin Elmer LS 55), UV–vis (Shimadzu UV1800 UV–vis spectrophotometer), X-ray diffraction (Bruker D8 Advance X-ray diffractometer), FT-IR (Bruker Vertex 70 FT-IR spectrometer) spectroscopy analyses, Zeta potential measurements, and quantum yield calculations. Transmission electron microscopy was used for imaging the carbon dots (TEM, Hitachi High Tech HT7700). The surface characteristics and composition of the carbon dots-coated chitosan or glass microbeads were studied through Scanning Electron Microscopy - Energy Dispersive X-ray analysis (SEM-EDX) (ZEISS EVO LS10). The fluorescent dye-labeled (rhodamine B isothiocyanate) glass microbeads were examined by fluorescence microscopy (OLYMPUS/BX53).

The quantum yield of carbon dots was calculated using a method reported elsewhere [29]. Quinine sulfate was used as a reference. The fluorescence emission of carbon dots was recorded at various excitation wavelengths 260–460 nm, pH (2.0–9.0), solvents (water, ethanol, DMSO, acetone, tetrahydrofuran (THF), n-hexane, and toluene), and salt concentrations (0.02–0.2 M NaCl).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots-coated glass microbeads
ce:section.ce:para: The glass microbeads were cleaned by sonication in ethanol, then dried at 70 °C and stored in a sealed container. The ethanol-washed glass microbeads (GD1) underwent a surface hydroxylating treatment in a mixture of concentrated sulfuric acid and H2O2 solution (piranha solution) [30]. First, the glass microbeads (10.0 g) in 12.0 mL of concentrated sulfuric acid were heated to the boiling temperature, and then 4.0 mL of H2O2 was added into the reaction vessel. The reaction medium was heated for 1 h at boiling temperature in a fume hood with ventilation. After the reaction was completed, the vessel was left to cool down at room temperature before being transferred to an ice bath. The remaining reaction mixture was then discarded. The treated microbeads were rinsed with distilled water and then dried in an oven at 100 °C to dehydrate their surfaces. Finally, the surface-activated glass microbeads (GD2) were kept in a sealed container.

The surface-activated glass microbeads were undergone a silylation reaction to introduce primary amino groups on their surface. In the amino-silane coupling reaction, the surface-activated glass microbeads (6.0 g) were treated with 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane (3.6 mL) in anhydrous toluene (12.0 mL) at the boiling temperature for 24 h in a fume hood cabinet. The amino-functionalized glass microbeads were collected and rinsed first with anhydrous toluene, then with ethanol, and finally with distilled water. The amino-functionalized glass microbeads (GD3) were dried in an oven at 70 °C and kept in a sealed container.

The amino-functionalized glass microbeads were treated with glutaraldehyde to crosslink the primary amines on their surface. In the cross-linking treatment, the amino-functionalized glass microbeads (5.0 g) were treated with glutaraldehyde (90 μL) in 90 mL of methanol at 70 °C for 24 h. Then, the surface-crosslinked glass microbeads (GD4) were recovered, rinsed with distilled water, dried in an oven at 70 °C, and kept in a sealed container.

A similar procedure was followed for the preparation of carbon dots-coated glass microbeads. Briefly, the amino-functionalized glass microbeads (GD3) (5.0 g) were treated with carbon dots (50 mg) and glutaraldehyde (90 μL) in 90 mL of methanol at 70 °C for 24 h. Then, the carbon dots-coated glass microbeads (GD5) were recovered, rinsed with distilled water, dried in an oven at 70 °C, and kept in a sealed container.

Rhodamine B (5 mg) in DMSO (5.0 mL) was used to label the amino-functionalized glass microbeads (GD3) (50 mg) before the fluorescence microscopy analysis. The amino-functionalized glass microbeads in the rhodamine B-DMSO mixture were heated at 50 °C for 1 h and rested in the reaction medium for 24 h. The labeling reaction and the incubation processes were performed in the dark. Finally, the fluorescent dye-labeled glass microbeads were examined by fluorescence microscopy. The glass microbeads (GD1–5) were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy and SEM-EDX analyses.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation of carbon dots-coated chitosan microbeads
ce:section.ce:para: Chitosan solution was prepared by dissolving chitosan (6.0 g) in acetic acid solution (2 %, 300 mL). The chitosan solution was stirred overnight to ensure the complete dissolution of chitosan. As droplets, the chitosan solution was added into the coagulation solution (methanol-water-NaOH solution; 300 mL:200 mL: 60 g). The chitosan gel microbeads formed in the medium were kept in the coagulation solution overnight. The chitosan gel microbeads were then filtrated, washed with distilled water for neutrality, and stored in water. One-third of the chitosan beads were dried at room temperature and called GD6.

Half of the remaining chitosan microbeads were collected and underwent the crosslinking reaction in a vessel containing glutaraldehyde (0.6 mL) and methanol (60 mL). The crosslinking reaction medium was heated at 70 °C for 24 h in a ventilated fume hood cabinet reflux system. The crosslinked chitosan microbeads were filtrated and washed with distilled water to remove unreacted glutaraldehyde molecules. Then, the crosslinked chitosan microbeads (GD7) were dried at room temperature.

The other half was used to prepare carbon dots-chitosan microbeads. The chitosan microbeads were put in the crosslinking reaction vessel containing carbon dots (100 mg), glutaraldehyde (0.6 mL), and methanol (60 mL). The reaction medium was heated at 70 °C for 24 h. The chitosan microbeads were collected by filtration and washed with distilled water to remove unreacted glutaraldehyde molecules and free carbon dots. Then, the carbon dots-chitosan microbeads (GD8) were dried at room temperature. The chitosan microbeads (GD6–8) were characterized using FT-IR spectroscopy and SEM-EDX analyses.

Preparation of bacterial cultures: For the antibacterial activity studies, we used four standard bacterial strains: two gram-negative bacteria (Escherichia coli, ATCC 25922 and Salmonella typhimurium ATCC 13311) and two gram-positive bacteria (Bacillus subtilis ATCC 6633 and Staphylococcus aureus ATCC 25923). These strains were supplied in frozen form and resuscitated in MHB before being cultured at 37 °C for 16–18 h at 120 rpm. The samples were then plated on Petri dishes with MHA, and the colony formations were observed. All bacterial suspensions used in the studies were prepared from these plates by taking them into MHB medium under aseptic conditions. Before the examinations, the microorganisms were passaged at least two times to confirm their purity and cell viability.

Glass (GD1–5) or chitosan microbeads (GD6–8) in varying amounts (32, 16, 8, 4, 2, 1 mg/well) were added to the wells of 96-well U-bottom microplates. Microplates were used in the antimicrobial studies after being sterilized under UV for 5 h. After sterilizing the microplates containing glass or chitosan microbeads coated/uncoated with carbon dots, 100 μL MHB with bacteria was added to the wells. In addition, control wells (blank) into which only the test materials were added to the system to check how the test materials react in a bacteria-free environment. The carbon dots were dissolved in the sterilized nutrient medium (MHB) at a 100 mg/mL stock concentration and sterilized by passing through a 0.22 μm filter. This stock was used for antimicrobial studies to create twelve test concentrations ranging from 25 mg/mL to 12.25 μg/mL, prepared through 1/2 serial dilution.

Ampicillin and tetracycline antibiotics were used as positive controls in the test system. Stock solutions were prepared by dissolving ampicillin in 0.8 M NaOH and tetracycline in distilled water. Twelve different concentrations were prepared through serial dilution in the medium using an initial 100 μg/mL concentration (CLSI M07-A9, 2012: 1–67; CLSI M02-A11, 2012: 1–58).

The test materials were used as microbeads (glass or chitosan, GD1–8) to increase the surface area that would interact effectively with the bacterial cells. That way, they were more suitable for the antibacterial experiment setup used in the study. In this way, the test materials were easily weighed during the experiments.

Bacterial suspensions with a density of 0.5 McFarland (1.5 × 108 cfu/mL) were prepared by passaging from the overnight cultures for the liquid microdilution test (CLSI M02-A11, 2012: 1–58). Bacterial solutions for the test were prepared at a density of 5.105 cfu/mL by diluting 1/200 of these cultures in the logarithmic phase and diluting with MHB. Bacterial suspensions (100 μL, 105 cfu/mL) were inoculated into the wells containing the glass and chitosan microspheres. Similarly, 50 μL of bacterial solutions were added to the wells with serial dilutions (50 μL/well) prepared in microplates containing carbon dots and antibiotics, with a final concentration of 105 cfu/mL.
Microplates were incubated for 24 h at 37 °C, and the MIC, MBC, and GI50 values for all bacteria were determined after 24 h of incubation (CLSI M02-A11, 2012: 1–58; CLSI M07-A9, 2012: 1–67).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of MIC, MBC, and GI50 values
ce:section.ce:para: The MIC values were determined by visually evaluating the turbidity in the wells. Once the incubation was completed, the amount of substance in the well before the previous well where growth occurred was noted as the MIC value for each material. In addition, MBC gives a substance concentration that can reduce 99.9 % of the number of bacteria present initially. Bacterial growth can continue even if there is no turbidity at the MIC concentration and below. For this reason, it was preferred to determine the concentrations of MBC to assess the antibacterial activity fully. To evaluate the MBC value, three concentrations with no turbidity were examined, beginning with the MIC concentration. 10.0 μL of sterile solution from the wells of these concentrations were taken and inoculated into MHA plates. The plates were incubated for 16–18 h at 37 °C, and at the end of the incubation, the plates were checked for any signs of bacterial growth. The concentration at which there was no growth was identified as the MBC.

To quantitatively analyze the growth densities at various concentrations in the microplate wells, 85.0 μL was taken from each well containing glass or chitosan microbeads after the incubation. This was then added to sterile new microplates in the same configuration. New microplates, which did not have glass or chitosan microbeads, were analyzed spectrophotometrically at 620 nm. On the other hand, the microplates of the carbon dots or antibiotics, which were used as positive controls in the experimental setup, were analyzed directly. The growth percentages were calculated by comparing the absorbance data with their respective controls based on the concentrations of the test materials. The S-probit software was used to calculate the GI50 values, corresponding to the point at the concentration when bacterial growth was 50 %. Thus, the antibacterial activities of the test materials used in the experimental setup were compared with each other and with the antibiotics (the positive control) qualitatively (MIC and MBC tests) and quantitatively (GI50 values).

Statistical analyses of the results obtained from the antibacterial effect studies were performed with GraphPad Prism 5.0. The results of treatment groups (GD1-GD8) and controls were compared collectively using the One-Way ANOVA for each bacterial strain, and it was tried to determine whether there was a significant difference within the group at the 0.05 significance level.

In addition, the groups formed with glass bead microbeads (GD1-GD5) and those formed with chitosan microbeads were evaluated within themselves with the One-Way ANOVA test. To see the effect of carbon dots on bacteria on glass and chitosan surfaces, surfaces with and without carbon dots were compared using Student's t-Test at a significance level of 0.05. In addition, the antibacterial activities of carbon dots added to chitosan and glass surfaces were compared statistically with Student's t-Test at the 0.05 significance level.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave-assisted synthesis of carbon dots from oleaster fruits
ce:section.ce:para: The optimal conditions for synthesizing carbon dots from the water extract of oleaster fruits were established: concentrated sulfuric acid/distilled water ratio 1.0/9.0, (mL/mL), microwave energy level (800 W), microwave irradiation time (5 min.). Carbon dots were analyzed using TEM and HR-TEM to determine their size and surface morphology (Fig. 1 ). They mainly were spherical, with sizes ranging below 50 nm, and dispersed well in water. Many of the particles were measured around 20 nm. HR-TEM image demonstrated that carbon dots have crystalline graphitic cores. The lattice spacing in the core structure of the carbon dots is visible in the HR-TEM image (Fig. 1, lower). The HR-TEM image reveals well-resolved lattice fringes with 216 pm spacing, corresponding to graphene's (100) in-plane lattice [31,32].

The size of carbon dots affects their optical features and how they interact with cells and bacteria. A previous study revealed that smaller carbon dots have more potent antibacterial effects, possibly due to differences in cell distribution or limitations in their interaction with functional groups and genetic material [33].

The FT-IR spectrum analysis revealed the presence of functional groups on the surface of the carbon dots: stretching vibration bands of –OH, –NH at 3303 cm−1, –CH2 at 2972–2888 cm−1, CC and CO at 1616 and 1700 cm−1, CH at 1377 cm−1, CN stretching 1082 and 1040 cm−1 and SC at 609 cm−1 [34] (Fig. 2 ).

Carbon dots' surface properties affect their solubility, optics, and interaction with metal ions, biomolecules, and biological entities [35,36]. Surface chemistry affects the antibacterial activity of carbon dots. For instance, N-doped carbon dots have higher bactericidal activity than S-doped ones [37]. The bactericidal effect of N-doped carbon dots is due to N-carrying functionalities on their surface. These functionalities facilitate the generation of active oxygen species/superoxide ions (O2−), causing damage to bacteria. In aqueous media, these functionalities protonate and disrupt bacterial cell membranes.

UV–visible spectroscopy was used to study the electronic transitions of carbon dots in pure water. The spectrum displayed a broad absorbance band in the UV–visible range, extending into the visible region, with two weak peaks at 273 nm and 351 nm (Fig. 3a). The peak at a shorter wavelength in the UV region corresponded to the π → π* electronic transitions of CC and CN. The shoulder at a longer wavelength is attributed to n → π* transitions of CO [38].

The carbon dots had strong blue fluorescence due to surface groups and defects. Their photoluminescence quantum yield was 0.11, with reference to quinine sulfate (Φ = 0.54). In pure water, they had excitation-dependent fluorescence (Fig. 3b) [39]. The photoluminescence of carbon dots was influenced by the pH level of their aqueous solutions (in the pH range of 2.0–9.0) (Fig. 3 c). The pH of the carbon dots solution was 3.75 without adjustment. Photoluminescence increased as pH decreased, with the highest emission at pH 2.0. In alkaline conditions, photoluminescence weakened with the lowest intensity at pH 9.0. Carbon dots are pH-sensitive due to surface functional groups. [40].

The study investigated the effect of different solutions on the fluorescence emission of carbon dots. Maximum fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots in the medium of water, ethyl alcohol, DMSO, acetone, THF, n-hexane, and toluene were recorded (Fig. 3d). The fluorescence emission intensity was more significant in water or ethyl alcohol and lowest in n-hexane. This showed that the fluorescence emissions of carbon dots depended on the medium in which they dissolve. Higher fluorescence emission intensity in highly polar solutions such as water and ethyl alcohol can be attributed to polar functional groups (carboxyl, hydroxyl, amino, and carbonyl, etc.) present on the surfaces of carbon dots. It was observed that the carbon dots did not dissolve well in low-polarity solutions like n-hexane and toluene. Earlier publications have reported that the fluorescence emissions of carbon dots can differ in various environments [41].

Carbon dots' fluorescence emission intensity remained unchanged in NaCl solutions of different concentrations (Fig. 3 e). However, the fluorescence intensity of carbon dots was previously reported to be affected by the ionic strength of KCl solutions [42].
According to the zeta potential measurements, the carbon dots in the water had an anionic character (−25.2 mV) due to the presence of surface functional groups like -OH and carboxylic groups [43].

XRD profiles reveal details on the carbon dots' core. A complex pattern with sharp peaks suggested a polycrystalline core rather than an amorphous one (Fig. 3 f). The peak at 17.50 could correspond to d-spacings of 0.51 nm. The significant spacings seem not determined randomly but could result from a connection between the lattices. The abundance of small peaks suggests that numerous slight variations could lead to similar yet distinct structures. The peak observed at 250 indicates a lattice spacing of 0.36 nm, suggesting the existence of a graphite-like structure that is not ideally arranged [44]. HR-TEM images also revealed the crystalline graphitic core of the carbon dots (Fig. 1). The HR-TEM image shows well-resolved lattice fringes with a spacing of 216 pm, corresponding to graphene's (100) in-plane lattice [31,32].

The carbon dots had a highly crystalline structure, which was evident from the sharp peaks in the XRD profile (see Fig. 3f). In a previous study conducted by our research team, we observed similar sharp crystalline peaks in the XRD profiles of carbon dots derived from mushrooms [29]. Additionally, some publications have reported similar findings in the XRD profiles of carbon dots [45].

Hot piranha solution activates the glass surface by breaking down Si–O–Si bonds, forming silanol groups [46]. The FT-IR spectra confirmed that the hot piranha solution, which is highly corrosive and oxidizing, was efficient at hydroxylating the glass surface by forming more silanol (Si–O–H) functionalities on the surface (Fig. 4a). The broadband, 3379 cm−1, arises from the stretching vibrations of –OH groups, and the Si–O–Si stretching bands at 1154–885 cm−1 from Si–O–Si and SiO. The 1638 cm−1 stretching band could be attributed to water molecules adsorbing onto the hydroxylated glass surface [47]. The piranha solution treatment made the glass surface more “active” and hydrophilic.

Next, the surface-activated glass microbeads underwent functionalizing treatment through silylation using an organoalkoxysilane coupling agent (APTES). Covalent siloxane (Si–O–Si) bonds are formed between the hydroxylated glass surface and APTES molecules through silylation. The Si atoms in APTES molecules can create one, two, or three distinct siloxane bonds with the glass surface [48]. The FT-IR spectrum of APTES-functionalized glass microbeads showed no –OH vibrational bands (Fig. 4 b). However, it did reveal the existence of –CH2 vibrational bands at 2900 cm−1, which directly result from the APTES molecules coupled on the surface. In the 1200–900 cm−1 range, there was a noticeable increase in the intensity of the bands (1005 cm−1) attributable to Si–O–Si vibrations. However, although amino groups were coupled to the surface by the covalent attachment of APTES molecules (by forming H2N–(CH2)3–Si–O–Si), the analysis did not fully reveal this. Yet, APTES-functionalized glass microbeads were treated with glutaraldehyde (Fig. 4 c). The FT-IR spectrum analysis did not reveal any observable bands related to the silicate structure of the glass on the surface of APTES-functionalized glass microbeads that were treated with either glutaraldehyde alone or in combination with carbon dots (Fig. 4 d). Therefore, a more powerful analysis method, SEM-EDX, was employed, as described in the following sections. Nevertheless, FT-IR spectrum analyses provided initial insights into the organic content and modifications on the surface.

Piranha solution activated the surface, forming channels on the glass microbeads (Fig. 5 a,b). The channels were filled with an organic layer during APTES functionalization, resulting in a smoother surface (Fig. 5 c,d). Glutaraldehyde further smoothed the surface (Fig. 5 e,f), and carbon dots were immobilized to form clusters (Fig. 5 g,h), indicating successful modification and immobilization. The microbeads were successfully amino-functionalized and labeled with a fluorescent dye (Fig. 6) [49].

The modification processes caused a change in the surface chemical composition of hydroxylated glass microbeads, with an accumulation of APTES, glutaraldehyde, and carbon dots on the inorganic glass surface. This was observed in SEM-EDX analyses, which showed increased C and N content due to the binding of carbon dots to the surface (Fig. S1).

Glutaraldehyde, a five‑carbon dialdehyde, is a crosslinker and fixative that reacts with primary amine groups. The treatment caused amino-functionalized glass microbeads to turn pink, and attaching carbon dots turned them brown (Fig. 7 ).

After the cross-linking treatment, the bands at 1645 and 1582 cm−1 in the FT-IR spectrum of non-crosslinked microbeads shifted to lower wave numbers. Additionally, changes in their intensity were observed, as shown in Fig. 8 a–c. Cross-linking chitosan with glutaraldehyde creates covalent bonding through an imine bond (–CH=N–) between the amine groups on chitosan and the aldehyde groups of glutaraldehyde. This bond is stabilized by resonance with the adjacent ethylenic double bond [50].

In the FT-IR spectrum of cross-linked chitosan microbeads, the intensity of the band at 1581 cm−1, which is attributed to ethylenic double bond formation, increased. This observation confirmed the imine bond formation and, thus, the chitosan cross-linked. In the FT-IR spectrum of carbon dots-coated microbeads, shifts, and new band formations were observed when compared with the FT-IR spectrum of cross-linked microbeads, especially in the bands between 1200 and 1600 cm−1 (CO, CC, CN). In addition, a band attributable to 2323 cm−1 –N=C=O was observed. This may be due to the interaction of carbon dots with chitosan fibers and the covalent binding of carbon dots to the chitosan surface [34]. SEM-EDX analyses were employed to study the morphology and chemical composition of the chitosan microbeads.

The SEM images revealed notable differences between the non-cross-linked chitosan microbeads and the cross-linked ones. The cross-linked chitosan microbeads differed significantly from non-cross-linked ones, exhibiting more defined shapes and formations of different sizes on their surfaces. However, cracks were present (Fig. 9 a-i). Covalently attaching carbon dots increased sphericity, resulting in a more uniform distribution. The deposited carbon dots were also visible on the surface.

Modifying chitosan microbeads changed their chemical composition. The SEM-EDX analysis of the chitosan microbeads revealed an increase in C and N-containing atomic groups on the surface of non-crosslinked chitosan microbeads following the immobilization of carbon dots (Fig. S2). The surface oxygen content of non-crosslinked chitosan microspheres was initially high but decreased with crosslinking and bonding of carbon dots. This suggests that the oxygen-containing groups remain in the inner parts after modification. The original color of chitosan microbeads was changed by cross-linking treatment and attachment of carbon dots to their surface (Fig. 10 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Antibacterial activities of carbon dots-coated chitosan and glass microbeads
ce:section.ce:para: Antibiotic resistance has become a common problem due to the excessive and long-term use of antibiotics in both clinical and agricultural practices. Bacteria can quickly adapt to environmental conditions that threaten them, including drugs. This is a complex issue, especially for treating infectious diseases. Therefore, there is a frequent need for new, innovative antibacterial agents that are not based on antibiotics or that can bypass bacterial antibiotic resistance mechanisms [51]. In recent years, nanomaterials with specific antibacterial activities have been discovered to play a new role in treating bacterial infections. Compared to larger molecules, nanomaterials, such as carbon dots, have been reported to exhibit bacteriostatic and bactericidal activities through photodynamic effects against antibiotic-resistant/sensitive bacteria [15,20–23].

The study aimed to assess the antibacterial properties of chitosan-coated and glass surfaces coated with carbon dots, as well as free carbon dots. To determine the contribution of carbon dots to the antibacterial activity of the surfaces, the chitosan and glass surfaces were prepared step-by-step and tested for antibacterial activity after each stage.

The antibacterial activities of the glass microbeads (GD1–5), chitosan microbeads (GD6–8), and the free carbon dots (GD9) (Table 1) were evaluated for against four bacterial strains – Gram-negative bacteria (E. coli, S. typhimurium) and Gram-positive bacteria (B. subtilis, S. aureus). The antibacterial activities of the test materials used in the experimental setup were compared with each other and with the antibiotics (the positive control) qualitatively (MIC, MBC) and quantitatively (GI50 values) (The data and the corresponding discussion on the antibacterial tests are presented in Supplementary Material, Figs. S3–36, Tables S1–11).

Also, routinely used antibiotics ampicillin and tetracycline were studied as positive controls in the experimental design. The effects of routinely used antibiotics against different bacterial species can vary, according to the literature reports (the range of MIC values is 1–32 μg/mL for ampicillin and ≤ 0.25–100 μg/mL for tetracycline) [52]. The study recorded MIC values for both antibiotics within these ranges (Table 2 ), indicating the correct experimental system design.

MIC and MBC values of the test materials and the positive controls are listed in Tables 2 and 3 and images of the microplates used in the antibacterial tests after the MIC test are given in Fig. 12. The cell viability (%) values at the highest concentration (32 mg/100 μL for G1-G8 and 25 mg/mL) for each bacterium are presented in Figs. 11 and 13 . The GI50 values could not be calculated for most test materials as their % cell viability values stayed below 50 % for more than one concentration. However, the GD3 sample had some concentrations close to 50 %, allowing for calculating its GI50 value even though its % cell viability values did not fall below 50 %. Previous studies revealed that amine-functionalized surfaces with APTES [53] or chitosan [54] can exhibit antibacterial properties. The primary antibacterial mechanism of the amino-functionalized surfaces (with APTES or chitosan) is through electrostatic interactions between protonated amino functionalities on the surface and negatively charged residues on bacterial cell membranes, which can lead to cell death by disrupting membrane integrity or permeability [53].

We obtained the following MIC values for the tested materials (GD1–GD9) as part of the antibacterial testing.
•
GD1, GD2, GD6: ˃32 mg/100 μL for all bacteria,
•
GD3, GD7, GD8: 32 mg/100 μL for all bacteria,
•
GD4, GD5: 32 mg/100 μL only in Gram-positive bacteria and higher than this value in Gram-negative bacteria,
•
GD9 was effective at 25 mg/mL against Gram-positive bacteria and higher concentrations against Gram-negative bacteria.

MBC indicates the initial bacterial concentration reduction by 99.9 %. Bacterial growth can occur even at concentrations below the MIC level without causing turbidity in the well. To evaluate the minimum bactericidal activity of the test material, MBC concentrations were determined. In this context, the test materials were compared based on their MBC values, another indicator of antibacterial efficacy.

The MBC tests revealed that
(i)
GD4 and GD5 exhibited bactericidal properties specifically against Gram-positive bacteria, with an MBC of 32 mg/100 μL,
(ii)
GD3 exhibited bactericidal and bacteriostatic effects on both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria at a concentration of 32 mg/100 μL,
(iii)
The other test materials (GD1, GD2, GD6, GD7, GD8, GD9) did not have a bactericidal effect on the bacteria.

Based on the % cell vitality values,
(i)
None of the tested GD1, GD2, GD4, GD5, and GD6 concentrations had an antibacterial effect on Gram-negative bacteria,
(ii)
GD3, GD6, and GD7 exhibit antibacterial effects against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria at a concentration of 32 mg/100 μL,
(iii)
Only GD6 did not have an antibacterial effect on Gram-positive bacteria, while all other materials did at the highest concentration,
(iv)
Compared to other test materials, GD5 had a more significant impact on Gram-positive bacteria,
(v)
At a concentration of 32 mg/100 μL, GD7 and GD8 exhibited antibacterial effects against all bacterial strains,
(vi)
GD9 was effective only against Gram-negative S. typhimurium and Gram-positive B. subtilis bacteria,
(vii)
All test materials showed significantly lower antibacterial effects than positive control antibiotics.

The antibacterial properties of carbon dots derived from oleaster fruit (GD9) were investigated in this study. The study also examined the effectiveness of carbon dots-bonded glass microbeads (GD5) and carbon dots-bonded chitosan microbeads (GD8) compared to their non-coated versions (GD4 and GD7). The goal was to analyze the performance of these microbeads and their ability to inhibit bacterial growth on glass or chitosan surfaces.

The antibacterial effects of surface-crosslinked glass microbeads (GD4) and carbon dots-coated glass microbeads (GD5) were compared at the minimum inhibition concentration (32 mg/100 μL). The results revealed that GD4 had a 27 % effect on the cell viability of only Gram-positive B. subtilis. However, GD5, formed by binding carbon dots to GD4, had a more significant impact on Gram-positive bacteria, with 35 % for B. subtilis and 43 % for S. aureus (p < 0.05). The addition of carbon dot coating significantly enhanced the antibacterial effects of the glass surface (p < 0.05). The glass microbeads without carbon dots had no antibacterial effect on S. aureus, but carbon dots attached to them decreased the cell viability for S. aureus by 43 %. This demonstrates that carbon dots attachment to glass microbeads can significantly enhance the antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria (p < 0.05). Carbon dots contributed to antibacterial activity against Gram-positive bacteria B. subtilis and S. aureus (p < 0.05), but against Gram-negative bacteria, E. coli, and S. typhimurium, the activity was not that much (p > 0.05).

It should be noted that plain chitosan microbeads (GD6), formed in an alkaline methanol-water solution, washed, and then dried, not crosslinked, did not show significant antibacterial activity (p > 0.05). Understanding the antibacterial mechanism of chitosan can be challenging due to various factors affecting its antibacterial properties. [55]. The antibacterial activity of chitosan is influenced by several factors, including the type of microorganism, chitosan's molecular weight (which determines the viscosity), deacetylation degree (which governs the number of amino groups), chitosan concentration, pH value, biological source of chitosan, and temperature [56]. In the study, low molecular weight chitosan was used to form plain chitosan microbeads. These beads have low surface area and are not soluble in aqueous media, which explains the observed lower antibacterial activity compared to chitosan solutions reported in earlier papers [57–59].

The study also aimed to compare the impact of cross-linked chitosan microbeads (GD7) and carbon dots-coated chitosan microbeads (GD8) on bacterial viability at the minimum inhibition concentration (32 mg/100 μL). The chitosan group (GD6–8) exhibited statistically significant antibacterial activity against all tested bacteria except for B. subtilis (p < 0.05). Carbon dots-bound chitosan microbeads were more effective at inhibiting the cell viability of E. coli, S. aureus, S. typhimurium, and B. subtilis compared to carbon dots-free chitosan microbeads. Specifically, the cell viability of E. coli was reduced by 27 %, S. aureus by 28 %, S. typhimurium by 20 %, and B. subtilis by 36 %. Therefore, carbon dots-bound chitosan microbeads show promise as a potential antimicrobial agent. The antibacterial effect on E. coli, S. typhimurium, S. aureus, and B. subtilis was boosted by 11 %, 0 %, 5 %, and 23 %, respectively, with the binding of carbon dots to chitosan microbeads. The study demonstrated that using carbon dots and chitosan microbeads together effectively increased the antibacterial efficacy against both Gram-positive and Gram-negative bacteria. Glutaraldehyde crosslinking and carbon dot immobilization modifications increased the antibacterial activity of chitosan microbeads against the bacteria strains, except against B. subtilis (p < 0.05).

A comparison was made between the antibacterial effects of carbon dots-coated glass microbeads (GD5) and carbon dots-coated chitosan microbeads (GD8) at the minimum inhibition concentration (32 mg/100 μL). The findings showed that carbon dots bound to glass microbeads only had an antibacterial effect on Gram-positive bacteria, reducing the cell viability of B. subtilis by 35 % and S. aureus by 43 %. However, carbon dots-coated chitosan microbeads had a broader antibacterial effect range, inhibiting Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria. It reduced the cell viability of E. coli by 27 %, S. typhimurium by 20 %, S. aureus by 28 %, and B. subtilis by 36 %. Carbon dots bound to chitosan microbeads increased the antibacterial effect on E. coli by 27 % and S. typhimurium by 20 %, respectively, but decreased it by 15 % for S. aureus. B. subtilis remained unaffected. According to the results, glass microbeads containing carbon dots (GD5) did not exhibit any antibacterial activity against Gram-negative bacteria (p > 0.05). On the other hand, chitosan microbeads with carbon dots exhibited low antibacterial activity (p < 0.05). Additionally, GD5 showed a more significant antibacterial effect against Gram-positive bacteria than GD8, as presented in Tables 2 and 3.

Although GD8 (carbon dots-coated chitosan microbeads) exhibited better antibacterial properties than GD6 (chitosan microbeads, not crosslinked) and GD7 (glutaraldehyde-crosslinked chitosan microbeads) (Table 3), the difference was not significant. The antibacterial effects of chitosan and carbon dots are typically attributed to their electrostatic interactions with bacterial cell walls [2,6,16,17]. Other factors like cell penetration and blocking cell transport can also be named, but in this study, chitosan was used as a solid surface rather than a solution. Carbon dots were immobilized on the surface of GD8, not free. This immobilization was achieved through covalent bonding of a chemical crosslinker (glutaraldehyde) between the surfaces. This may explain the moderate antibacterial activity of GD8 observed in the study.

We also tested the antibacterial effect of carbon dots in free form (dispersed in an aqueous medium) (GD9) and found that it depended on concentration. Carbon dots were effective (25 mg/mL) against Gram-positive B. subtilis bacteria and at higher concentrations against Gram-negative S. typhimurium.

Their cell wall characteristics determine the susceptibility of bacteria to antibacterial agents. Gram-negative bacteria are typically more resistant, which leads to increased pathogenicity [60]. Carbon dots have been widely studied as bactericidal materials in recent years. The antibacterial mechanism of carbon dots differs from conventional antibiotics. It involves ROS (reactive oxygen species) generation, cell structure degeneration, cytoplasm leakage, and DNA binding with gene expression modulation [61].

Although there have been different evaluations of the antibacterial mechanisms of chitosan and carbon dots, their clear mechanism of action is not yet established. However, the most probable and important mechanism is that they induce cell death in bacteria by generating reactive oxygen species (ROS) in the cell. Available data suggests that both materials primarily act outside the cell, leading to the disruption of the bacterial cell wall and cell membrane. This phenomenon is mainly attributed to the charged nature of these particles that interact with biological components, leading to the disruption of the cell wall and membrane integrity. After carbon dots gain intra-cellular access through cell wall damage, little is known about their mechanism of antibacterial activity. Cellular components such as enzyme systems and gene expression are likely affected by carbon dots [62].

The surface charge state of carbon dots significantly affects their electrostatic attraction to microbial cells. Carbon dots with positively charged surfaces enhanced antibacterial performance in some studies [63,64]. In this study, carbon dots derived from oleaster fruits had a net negative charge. However, they exhibited antibacterial activity against Gram-positive B. subtilis and Gram-negative S. typhimurium. On the other hand, previous studies have suggested the size and shape of carbon dots can influence their interaction with bacterial cell membranes and, therefore, their effectiveness against bacteria [65]. Although microbeads cannot enter bacterial cells due to their large size, carbon dots can diffuse into the cells because of their nanoscale size. Carbon dots of small size can easily enter the cell membrane, leading to increased ROS stress [61]. In this study, the carbon dots were nearly spherical but varied in size, with most particles measuring around 20 nm or less (Fig. 1). Therefore, it was not easy to make explicit comments regarding the impact of size on antibacterial activity. Although carbon dots and microbeads have potential antibacterial effects, additional studies are needed to understand their combined mechanism of action.

This study revealed (i) carbon dots with a negative charge can be synthesized by exposing the water extract of oleaster fruit to microwave radiation, (ii) glutaraldehyde can immobilize carbon dots on glass and chitosan surfaces, (iii) carbon dots dispersed in an aqueous medium have an antibacterial effect on both Gram-negative S. typhimurium and Gram-positive B. subtilis bacteria, (iv) carbon dots on the surface of glass microbeads can selectively enhance the antibacterial properties against Gram-positive bacteria (B. subtilis, S. aureus), (v) the carbon dots-chitosan microbeads can exhibit a more potent antibacterial effect compared to the chitosan microbeads, (vi) the carbon dots-chitosan microbeads exhibit antibacterial properties against both Gram-negative and Gram-positive bacteria (E. coli, S. typhimurium, S. aureus, B. subtilis), (vii) and the carbon dots-chitosan microbeads exhibit superior antibacterial properties compared to carbon dots-glass microbeads.

The glass microbeads coated with carbon dots showed a more potent antibacterial effect on Gram-positive bacteria when compared to the chitosan microbeads coated with carbon dots. However, the overall evaluation of the data reveals that bonding carbon dots to chitosan microbeads was more effective than bonding to glass microbeads, as carbon dots attached to chitosan microbeads exhibited antibacterial effects at varying rates in all bacteria tested in the study.

This paper reported the antibacterial properties of carbon dots-coated chitosan surfaces against only four bacterial strains. In further studies, the following points should be addressed.
1-
The potential applications of carbon dots-coated chitosan surfaces,
2-
The antifungal properties of carbon dots-coated chitosan surfaces,
3-
The mechanism behind the antibacterial/antifungal activity of free and bound carbon dots,
4-
Immobilization of carbon dots on various carbohydrate polymeric surfaces, e.g., cellulose surfaces.
This study was funded by Selcuk University Research Foundation, Konya, Turkey (BAP-23401088).

Yanar Najmalden Ghaibullah Ghaibullah: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation, Writing – original draft. Naciye Ozdemir: Data curation, Formal analysis, Investigation. Fatma Zilifdar Foto: Data curation, Formal analysis. Gulsin Arslan: Conceptualization, Supervision, Writing – review & editing.
